videos.summary: OpenAI introduces two new real-time audio models via its API: GPT Realtime Translate for live, 70-language translation, and GPT Realtime 2 for intelligent voice agents capable of reasoning, tool calling, and maintaining conversational context. The video demonstrates both models, showcasing their ability to handle interruptions, technical terms, and integration with external systems like CRMs, positioning voice as a primary interface.

videos.summary: The video announces Gemma 4, a new family of open-source models from Google DeepMind, available under an Apache 2.0 license. Designed for local inference on personal devices (phones, laptops, desktops, IoT), Gemma 4 includes 26B MoE, 31B dense, 2B, and 4B models. Key features highlighted are its agentic capabilities for complex logic and multi-step planning with native tool use, a large context window (up to 1/4 million tokens), and for smaller models, multimodal support (audio and vision) and multilingual capabilities. The announcement emphasizes rigorous security protocols for enterprise use
videos.title: What’s new in Gemma 4?

videos.summary: The video introduces DeepSeek V4, a new open-weight AI model described in a 58-page research paper. It features a 1 million token context window and employs three magical compression techniques (token-level, heavily compressed attention, and compressed sparse attention) to reduce KV-cache memory needs by 90%. DeepSeek V4 Pro's performance rivals frontier models like Google's Gemini 3.1 Pro, especially in recall and coding tasks, while being significantly more computationally efficient and cheaper to use. The speaker highlights its limitations, such as being unimodal and experiencing performanc
videos.title: DeepSeek V4 AI Beats Billion Dollar Systems…For Free
videos.transcript_text: Finally, DeepSeek 4 is here, and it is  described in a 58-page research paper. And finally, nothing is held back here. What am I even reading? How is that  even possible? Dear Fellow Scholars, this is Two Minute Papers  with Dr. Károly Zsolnai-Fehér! Well, it does three things that  are absolutely magical. One: Compression. Namely, compression for the KV  cache - this is a scratch pad where you write your prompts and add your documents.  Imagine reading a book. You can find answers so much quicker if you compress  each paragraph down into one sentence. You keep the book. But now you can search it  faster. They call it token-level compression. But even these little summaries add up. What do we do? Well, two. We want to  know the overall plot of the James Bond book? See if it’s one that  we read already? Well, of course, we look at the table of contents. If each  chapter has a short name, we can grasp the whole story from that tiny piece of information.  The paper describes it as a 128-to-1 compression. They call it Heavily Compressed Attention.  Now, the AI sees the whole story at a glance. But scientists at DeepSeek say this is still  not enough compression. We need more! Three. Imagine that we want to search for a fight  in the book. Table of contents helps a bit, but may not tell us exactly where the fights  are. So, we look at an index. A list of words and phrases and their locations. Okay, so looking  for a fight, and bingo! The index gives us the top 5 pages that have fights in them. This is genius,  and they call it Compressed Sparse Attention. So, three layers of compression:  summaries, structure, index. And suddenly, the three pieces click together. These three  reduce memory needs for the KV-cache by about 90%. I had to look twice. Down about 90%.  Squashing down a 100 words into a storage space of 10? And you are saying that we are not  losing basically every piece of information? Yes. That is exactly what they are  saying. But we are Fellow Scholars here, we look at proofs and experiments. Now just to make sure, this is KV-cache  compression. You still need to load the whole model. So it does not mean that  you can load the full DeepSeek Pro AI onto a toaster. Just want to make sure you know  that because media headlines and hype…you know. And now…hold on to your papers Fellow  Scholars, because this one delivers. They tested it by hiding 8 facts inside  increasingly long contexts. So how good is it? Well, they report that the Pro  version recalls it better than Gemini 3.1 Pro. That is Google’s flagship  product. Wow. That is unbelievable. But note that like many other systems, it starts  to degrade as you start approaching the limits of the context window. Then, models forget.  Drift. Hallucinate. More text means less truth. Also, let’s look at its accuracy versus  the previous DeepSeek, especially since this new version is heavily compressing  things. Ha. Look at that. This is crazy. It is also fantastic at coding. If you are a  coder, great. If you are not a coder, well, you are now. It is so easy to ask it to generate  javascript code that you can paste into a website and run, and in some cases, you can even  run programs in the DeepSeek window with one click. I am a light transport researcher  by trade, that is ray tracing if you will, so I had to try a little coding task related to  that and…this is fantastic. It still failed to properly implement more advanced algorithms so I  am excited to see what the next version brings. It is crushing benchmarks…and the competition. At  the low-low price of… free. If you can self-host it yourself, hardware is pricey, they also  provide online access to it and it is so cheap, I feel like numbers are losing their  meaning. Soon, intelligence will get too cheap to meter. Depending on  whether there is a discount or not, you can easily get pricing that is  30 times cheaper than Anthropic’s Claude. Even with no discount, things  can get 8 to 20 times cheaper. Crazy. Now, let’s temper expectations a bit. Limitations.  That’s what is missing from the media headlines. One, you can almost hear the 1,500 pages  fluttering as it churns through it. But wait. I did not say also 10 hours of audio, or full  feature-length movie. There is a reason for that. This system is unimodal. Not multimodal. No images  or audio. It is blind and deaf, if you will. Two, this system is not fully understood, not even  by its creators. They report two techniques that magically stabilize training, and they say that  they are not quite sure why. I’ll note that this is something that happens to every researcher, and  I have nothing but respect for the transparency. And three, we noted that if you are pushing  against the limits of the context window, things break down a bit. Be careful. Just want to make sure that you don’t  get oversold on what is going on here, this still has limitations. Not small ones. But, overall…this is not a small  step in open and free AI systems. Congratulations to the team and thank you so much. Now here’s what I think. I think this is a great  release and a great paper and great life advice too. Why? Well, you can adapt so many of these  ideas to your thinking. Imagine walking in the forest. You want to look at the amazing  views in front of you. But then, you trip. Or you look mainly in front of your feet so you  don’t trip. You watch your step… or you enjoy the view. Not both. So what is the solution? You do both. Scan near, glance far. Step and look. Local detail, global context. It is the  same as what DeepSeek does. Try it out next time you are on a walk, it’s weird. You’ll see. Let me  know in the context, I mean comments how it went. They also use a technique  called Engram - normally, an AI recalculates nearly every fact from  scratch every time. Engram lets it just recall those facts instead. It’s not as easy  as it sounds, we have a separate video on it, link in description. And we are still  just scratching the surface here. Now this is a really advanced research  paper, with all the good and the bad, not just the hype. Also, this video was not  super fast, I rewrote this over and over again. Why is that? Because distilling complex ideas  into simple explanations takes time. You get fewer views than others who publish something  as quickly as possible. But that’s what I try to do here, and it is an honor to do this  for such an incredibly smart and receptive audience like you Fellow Scholars. And thank  you so much for appreciating it - this one really made my day. Subscribe and  hit the bell if you enjoyed this.

videos.summary: Clay, a growth tool, uses AI agents extensively for tasks like web research, data synthesis, and outbound campaigns, running approximately 300 million agent runs per month. They adopted AI early, observing user behavior to develop their 'Claygent' web research agent. Clay is model-agnostic, using a metaprompter to map prompts to different models based on use case and user preference. They leverage LangSmith for two primary functions: profiling production traffic (monitoring usage, cost, errors, latency, and quality) and for accelerating zero-to-one agent development by visualizing traces. Key
videos.title: How Clay manages 300M agent runs a month with LangSmith
videos.transcript_text: So we think about Clay as finding, closing, and growing your best customers. And I would say for each of the verbs in that sentence, we have agents for those. Welcome everyone my name is Harrison,co-founder and CEO of LangChain, joined here by Jeff Barg at Clay. Jeff, do you want to introduce yourself briefly? Yeah. Well, thanks so much, Harrison. I'm Jeff. I lead our AI team at Clay and I'm really excited to chat. Alright, so most people probably have some idea of what Clay is, but for those that don't, do you maybe want to give an introduction of what Clay does and maybe how they're using AI throughout it? So Clay, we think of ourselves as the creative tool for growth. And really what that means is building any kind of growth idea in Clay. And, what that looks like in practice is building lists of companies and people enriching them with data points either through traditional enrichment providers or using AI for web research or data synthesis, et cetera and then activating on those lists of companies and people, either for outbound campaigns or lead enrichment, lead routing, things like that. So you guys were around well before AI came out. You were one of the first I think, to embrace it. Do you maybe want to talk about why you embraced it and how early you embraced it and what that process looked like from the early days up until where it is now? I think we started as effectively a chat completions wrapper, right? So people would use AI for: summarizing long documents or, you know, writing outbound copy. And I think one of the things that we observed early on was, people were actually using web research, so pulling down scraping web pages and then deterministically using an AI to summarize or extract insights from those webpages. And so I'd say that we were quite early in actually launching Claygent, which is our AI web research agent. And when did that launch? It might have been...mid 2023. Okay Yeah so like right after ChatGPT kind of Yeah, so, pretty early days in the, kind of, the GPT-4 era. And, yeah, I think what was interesting about that was, it was really a user behavior that we observed. So people actually kind of trying to wrap that into something that would actually do it autonomously, or agentically. And so nowadays, if someone uses Clay, like how many different touchpoints are there with AI? So we think about Clay as finding, closing and growing your best customers. And I would say for each of the verbs in that sentence, we have agents for those. So we have an agentic search product to actually do TAM sourcing and find your best customers to actually search through the web or search through our database of companies and people to find your best accounts, grow those accounts so doing research or actually figuring out whether a company meets your ICP with reasoning and sort of doing that agentically and then actually engaging with enclosing those accounts so either through a native sequencer or actually, you know, sending outbound messaging. And what is the scale that you guys are operating agents at? So I think, what's super interesting, I think about AI within our product is it's grown, of course because the business has grown, but, it's also grown more as a percentage of our overall actions over time. And, today we're running around 300 million agent runs a month. Okay. Quite large scale. And what is an agent run? How many steps does it take? How long does it run for? I'm sure it varies to some extent, but like what stats do you have there? Yeah it's grown over time as well. I think this is partly because of the underlying models, but also because of  our architecture. Somewhere between, I would say 10 to 30 steps on average doing a mix of, you know, synthesizing, web documents, actually searching the web, crawling different pages, things like that. As you guys started building more and more of these agents and, you know, Clay turned from purely kind of like a software app into this software plus agent app, how did your development process change for building those agents? We think about three things at Clay. So we think about the quality of the agent runs, so actually people care a lot about getting correct data and, you know, there's all sorts of idiosyncrasies about how people think about quality. But we think about quality, we think about throughput. So how many of these can we run in an efficient way? And we care about cost. And so I think we've used tools like LangSmith to really, do offline evaluations of where we are in quality and benchmark those against kind of where we are in a cost throughput, position. How do you measure quality for these runs? We have a mix of different methods. Some of the evals that we have are just purely factual, right? So just is the agent actually able to get the right answer and compare those against ground truth? And is that like a programmatic match or, I imagine if you're doing research, you're kind of like compiling like a paragraph at least of like an answer. So do you use some like LLM-as-a-judge to score that? Or what does that look like? We do yes. So some of these are just, we look for deterministic matches and we structure outputs and sort of, do it that way. And for some of these we do use LLM-as-a-judge. I'd say a lot of our Clay customers will also use these agents for combining both research and then actually synthesizing that research into a recommendation, whether that's qualification or outbound messaging. But you know they're sort of doing, it's a multipurpose sort of agent. When a new model comes out, and it seems like there's a new model coming out every week or something like that, what does your process look like to see if it's better or we'll talk about cost later on but what, does your process look like to see if it's a good fit for your type of workflows? I think Clay's position really is we we're model agnostic in some ways. So we do allow users to select their preference of, you know, model provider and I think we're not really yet at the point where models are completely interchangeable or, you know, they do have different writing styles, profiles of the actual work that they can do and I think users have come to learn that over time, right? So we have users that really prefer Anthropic models and users that really prefer OpenAI models for different things. I think our job is really, sort of giving the right recommendations to users. And so for us, we have a metaprompter tool that basically automatically maps prompts to different models based on the profile that we think is best for that underlying question. So when a new model comes out, do you add like a new mapping to that metaprompter tool? Is that what it looks like to support it? Exactly. Yeah. And I think we use kind of evals to understand where we should be mapping those things and for what use cases those models are best for. You've mentioned LangSmith a few times. How does that fit throughout the whole kind of like agent development process for you guys? For us, LangSmith is we really think about it in two ways. One is just profiling production traffic and understanding the patterns there, understanding if they're very different from what we expected or very widely across customers, things like that. And I think that, you know, LangSmith really helps us at our scale where it's impossible for us to know for every customer exactly what's going on. And when you say patterns, is this usage patterns or cost patterns, or errors, latency, or all of the above? All of the above, yeah. And I think, something that's really important for us is, being able to go to a customer level and understand what's happening. We get a lot of feedback from customers and it's really hard to know what's just happening on the ground and LangSmith really helps with just like knowing, sort of, hey a customer reports this, but here's what actually happened. I would even add to that list just sort of underlying quality, right? If someone, you know, if someone says there's a problem with the actual answers that our agents are outputting, being able to understand, like, was that a tool problem? Was that an inference problem, just kind of where in the stack that actually fell down. I think most people listening have some sense of this, but maybe to go into it, why can't you just know that before you go to production? There's a lot of things that can go wrong in production and I think, definitely on the quality side, I think the range of things that people are using agents for is just super broad And the input to them, is it just pure natural language for you guys? It is. Yeah. I think in most of the agents that we build that, there might be some structuring of, what we, you know, end up passing to the agent, but it's usually a blank text box and people can put anything in a blank text box. You were talking about kind of like production insights being one part of how you use LangSmith and the second part is? The second part is zero to one agent building. So, anytime we're, you know, we're building a lot of different agents across the go-to-market stack now so things like agentic search, things like account agents that are able to reason about different accounts in your CRM and for all of these new agents we really need to understand when you're building them, what's happening, just to get a kind of visualization of what those traces are and like what the outputs look like. And I think without that it's really hard to understand what's happening, right? Because like you just see the, kind of what you expose to the end user, which isn't the entire agent trace. So how early on in the development process do you turn on tracing? It's day zero. Yeah. So we're it's almost always one of the first things that we do and I think being able to stream in kind of traces, and actually it's part of the iteration process. It's just actually looking at the LangSmith traces. We ended up working together on LangSmith for this. Why use LangSmith as opposed to build it in-house or any other alternatives out there? I think LangSmith feels like a platform that was built by people who have built an agent framework. And I think that really shines through. So we really liked kind of the ability to get sort of quick stats or be able to see traces in a way that is able to understand the nesting of different subagents or different LLM paths as part of tool calls. And so there's all these sorts of small idiosyncrasies that really shine through in LangSmith. And, so you mentioned building your own agent harness, from, kind of like scratch. What did the process look like for connecting that to LangSmith? Yeah, so we built on top of the Vercel AI SDK and it was quite easy, right? So, it was actually, I think more or less a one line change to get traces exported to LangSmith. That's why it makes it so easy to start on day zero, it's quite a simple integration. Awesome. Any features of LangSmith that ended up being particularly critical, either like, that you thought ahead of time as you were evaluating or in the year or so since you've started using it? Yeah, we care a lot about cost observability and so I think that always was a key piece of what we were looking for. I think, UI/UX was actually like a pretty important part to us. Just being able to understand traces really quickly. We have a lot of engineers now working on different AI products in the company, and so being able to onboard them quickly, and effectively to the platform was an important piece. I'd say the last piece is sort of, being able to actually export traces to Snowflake and just be able to kind of do a lot of different analysis on these traces over time. I'll give one more: I think also just speed and performance of the platform is really crucial at our scale and so just being able to get traces in quickly the reliability of ingest, all of those kind of features. Out of curiosity, how many people at Clay have access to LangSmith? I'd say it's probably on the order of 25 to 50. And is it mostly engineering or are there people from other teams as well? It's still mostly engineering. I think we do have folks who are not typically working in AI features that do have access to LangSmith. I'd say some folks on our customer support team have started to use LangSmith for debugging, customer traces, and that's pretty cool. I think that was part of our initial evaluation was actually wanting to onboard more of the team over time to LangSmith. If you think about quantifying the impact that LangSmith has had, how would you do that? Are there any like, hard metrics you can point to? Are there any yeah how would you do it? The one metric that we really cared about was actually just cost reconciliation. And, we were in a pretty, I wouldn't say bad spot before LangSmith, but we, really didn't have a good sense of where we were spending money on different inference providers. With LangSmith, we now have, I think it's around like a 99 maybe like 99.5% reconciliation rate between the LangSmith data and our actual bills that we get from inference providers. So it's pretty much a hundred percent of coverage of what where we're actually spending money. Our finance team loves us for that. So I think that's always helpful. Yeah, I think like there are a bunch of intangibles of LangSmith as well. I think the development time, some of the tracing and just sort of, you know, more traditional observability features they're very helpful for us. I think it's you know downtime is erodes customer trust. Is bad. You guys, I think, are at the forefront of building a lot of agents, and we've seen the types of agents that are in production change over the past, you know, year, six months even, last three months. As agents become more and more viable and more and more agentic are there any features in LangSmith that have become more important when moving to that agentic world? And another selfish question, are there features that we should build that you think will be even more important in that kind of like future? One thing we're seeing more and more of and probably not surprising, but is longer-running agents. So agents that are taking many more steps and potentially running for longer periods of time. So, you know, we're seeing these longer-running and longer horizon agents. I think one of the things that's helpful in LangSmith is just being able to kind of, thread those over time. So the threads feature is quite helpful. I think just being able to, you know, again, like understand the underlying ground truth of what's happening in those agents is quite helpful. One other piece that I think we're seeing more of and we're experimenting with more is subagents. Yeah. I think a feature in LangSmith that is quite helpful is just being able to actually see the all these agent traces kind of together in one place. One of the things we're seeing is that, you know, coding agents are doing more and more work and helping build some of these agents. One of the things we're thinking about is basically giving more of a way to pull down traces and give those to coding agents so they can see what went wrong in production or in a run, and then fix themselves. Have you experimented with any of these, like self-healing ish workflow type things? We're starting to do more of just background agents. So inspired by the folks at Ramp, just sort of trying to build more coding agents is part of our SDLC. We haven't yet kind of closed the loop with LangSmith traces or actually production traces on that front, but I think it's a great idea. Aside from kind of like pushing what agents look like, I think one of the things that is unique about you guys is the scale at which you operate. What advice would you have for others who, you know, might not be at that scale now, but will hopefully get there in the next few months or year or so? So, yeah, I think one of the things that really helps when you're scaled out or you have agents that are at scale, is just being able to understand the different types of questions and different types of behaviors that your agents are exhibiting. And I think it's really easy to do that when you're at a small scale or, you know, you're rolling out a zero to one product, but as soon as you go into, you know, hundreds of thousands or millions of agent runs, you can't actually understand the ground truth or you can't actually understand what everyone is doing with agents at all times. I think, we've played around with sort of the insights product, with LangSmith and trying to understand just what are the patterns of different user behavior and different things that agents are doing. I think the other thing that's pretty helpful here is actually being able to have online evaluators for understanding what behavior of agents is happening, at scale. And so, you know, you might be able to get away with it, without having this, when you're in more of a zero to one phase and just building out your initial product. But certainly at Clay's scale those things are super helpful. Because in the zero to one phase you can look at everything manually. But you can't do that at scale. Exactly. Yeah. Or you can just talk to every customer and kind of like, you know, at some point that breaks down. As agents get more complex, one of the things that we've heard people talking about is memory and how that ties into to guiding them over longer and longer time horizons. Have you guys experimented with that at all or have any takes there? We're definitely seeing agents that are running over longer time horizons and, you know, potentially are running around the same task for, you know, several iterations over time. We haven't really seen a ton of examples of where the memory tools today actually benefit those use cases. So I think there's a lot of talk about continual learning or actually, you know, having agents improve over time if they're doing the same task. And I think a lot of that hasn't borne out yet right so like a lot of the memory tools of today haven't really hit product market fit. Are there techniques that maybe aren't in the memory tools of today that you have found to be useful? We've experimented with file systems and just sort of being able to have agents write to write-only logs, append-only logs over time. That's interesting and I think we're pretty excited about seeing where that goes. I'd say it's still pretty early. Yeah. One of the interesting things that we think about is, you know the traces in LangSmith obviously have all that record of kind of like what happened and the feedback associated there. And so we haven't done anything with this aside from like examples, but that can serve as a very interesting kind of like, you know, system of record to inform some of the memory updates. Like I think the skills paradigm is quite interesting and I think being able to go from a lot of traces into kind of skills to improve those over time feels like maybe the right pattern here. All right well thank you Jeff for joining me. really excited to have you here and I've enjoyed working with you on everything around LangSmith, and thank you for sharing all your wisdom with everyone who's watching. I appreciate it Harrison and thanks so much, appreciate the partnership.

videos.summary: Yannic Kilcher demonstrates a "fully automated mansplainer" built using a sequence of AI models (Whisper, Mistral Medium, VIVE Voice) running entirely on an NVIDIA DGX Spark. The video highlights the DGX Spark's capabilities for local inference, running large open-weight models (like GPT-OSS 120B and Gemma 3 4B), and enabling fine-tuning and experimentation. Kilcher emphasizes the device's benefits for users valuing privacy and autonomy, as well as tinkerers, due to its powerful hardware (120 GB unified RAM) and developer-friendly tools like AI Workbench and playbooks for various AI tasks.

videos.summary: The interview features Alexander Shoshenko, Head of Applied AI Research at Ramp, discussing the development of Ramp Sheets, an AI agent for spreadsheet editing. He details Ramp's journey from process mining internal finance tasks using Loom videos to generating documentation and attempting codegen, ultimately building an agentic system that directly modifies spreadsheets with Excel formulas. The discussion highlights the importance of transparency for finance professionals and touches on experiments with recursive language models and steering vectors.
videos.title: How Ramp built an AI agent that can think outside of tokens | Alex Shevchenko

videos.summary: The video showcases the 'Experience AI' class, designed to teach students the foundations of AI. It highlights students' curiosity and questions about AI, including large language models, data requirements, and bias. The program aims to engage students and equip them to be future problem-solvers in the field of AI.
videos.title: Teaching the foundations of AI in the classroom
